data.title: Markets brace for volatility as Strait of Hormuz tensions return
data.description:
Investors are preparing for a volatile start to the week after renewed tensions around the Strait of Hormuz raised doubts about last week’s strong rally in stocks and pullback in oil prices.

Iran warned over the weekend that ships approaching the strategic waterway could be treated as violating the ceasefire, while reports said Revolutionary Guard forces fired on commercial vessels. Tehran also signaled it would skip a second round of talks with the United States while a U.S. naval blockade remains in place.

The developments rattled optimism that had helped push the S&P 500 to a record high last week. Brent crude had fallen sharply Friday on hopes of easing tensions, while bonds rallied and the dollar gave back gains tied to the conflict.

“It appears that investors may have celebrated too early,” Martin Hennecke of St. James’s Place said, warning that recent market gains could be partially reversed.

President Donald Trump added to uncertainty Sunday by threatening to destroy Iranian infrastructure if negotiations fail. He also said a U.S. Navy vessel had seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship.

Analysts said the market remains vulnerable because the Strait of Hormuz, a key route for global oil and liquefied natural gas exports, has not fully reopened. Shipping disruptions, elevated tanker rates and tighter inventories could keep pressure on energy prices and inflation even if diplomacy resumes.

Bond markets have also shown caution, with two-year Treasury yields rising as traders reduced expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts this year.

data.title: Walmart tests store back rooms to speed marketplace deliveries: FT
data.description:
Walmart  (WMT) is testing a new strategy in its competition with Amazon (AMZN) by using storage space in store back rooms to speed delivery of products sold by third-party marketplace sellers, the Financial Times reported Sunday.

The retailer’s online marketplace offers roughly 500 million items from outside merchants, who keep ownership of goods until they sell and pay Walmart commissions and shipping fees.

Traditionally, these sellers store inventory at their own facilities or Walmart (WMT) warehouses. Orders shipped from Walmart (WMT) sites usually arrive within one or two days, while direct shipments from sellers can take longer.

Now Walmart (WMT) is experimenting with holding select marketplace products inside stores, allowing same-day delivery in some cases, the FT reported, citing people familiar with the effort.

The pilot is underway in several Dallas-area stores, a market Walmart has often used to test new ideas.

Walmart (WMT) executives see their vast store network as a major ecommerce edge because locations close to customers can double as delivery hubs. The company has said more than one-third of store-delivered online orders arrive in under three hours.

Recent supply-chain upgrades have helped free space for the new initiative. Walmart (WMT) has automated warehouse operations so merchandise arrives at stores pre-sorted for shelves, creating extra room in back areas.

The company is also using artificial intelligence to decide which marketplace products should be stocked in each store based on local demand.

Walmart’s marketplace business remains much smaller than Amazon’s (AMZN) but is growing quickly. Chief Financial Officer John David Rainey said last week that marketplace revenue is increasing about 20% annually.

Analysts say bringing third-party inventory closer to shoppers could improve delivery speeds, though limited back-room space may constrain how far the model can expand.

data.title: Vitol tells banks Q1 profit near $2B amid Iran market turmoil
data.description:
Vitol Group has told lenders it expects roughly $2 billion in first-quarter profit, as the world’s largest energy trader seeks to reassure banks after losses in parts of its business tied to the Iran conflict, Bloomberg News reported Sunday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The figure was described informally in recent discussions and could still change as the company finalizes quarterly results.

The Middle East conflict sent oil and gas prices sharply higher in early March, disrupting global cargo flows and stranding some shipments owned by Vitol in the Persian Gulf.

Bloomberg reported earlier this month that Vitol was reorganizing its derivatives desk after traders were caught on the wrong side of early wartime price swings. Some positions were closed, while others remained in place, though later trading reportedly recovered part of the losses.

Vitol has told banks that other divisions performed strongly and that the company retains solid liquidity, the people said.

Extreme volatility in commodity markets often benefits major trading houses. Vitol earned about $37 billion over 2022 through 2024 during the energy crisis triggered by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

The privately held company does not publicly release earnings, but it regularly updates lenders because commodity merchants depend heavily on large credit facilities to finance shipments worldwide.

People familiar with the matter said Vitol’s full-year profit declined sharply last year from the record $8.7 billion posted in 2024, though it still remained historically strong.

Several rivals, including Gunvor and trading arms of BP, Shell and TotalEnergies, have recently pointed to strong gains from the Iran-driven market upheaval.

data.title: Zelenskiy calls for European missile shield as Ukraine seeks faster air defense options
data.description:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said Sunday that Europe should develop its own system to defend against ballistic missile attacks, adding that Ukraine is already in discussions with several countries about launching such a project.

The proposal reflects one of Kyiv’s most urgent military needs, as Russian ballistic missile strikes continue to target Ukraine’s power grid and other critical infrastructure. Intercepting those weapons has largely depended on the U.S.-made Patriot system, whose missiles remain in limited supply.

"I believe, and my idea is that we should have a European anti-ballistic missile defense system. We are in talks with several countries and are working in this direction," Zelenskiy told Ukraine’s national Marathon television channel.

"We need to build our own anti-ballistic missile defense system within a year," he added.

Zelenskiy said the goal would be difficult but achievable, and noted he has already discussed the idea with several major European nations, though he did not identify them.

Ukraine and its partners have been searching for alternatives as Patriot inventories tighten amid heavy deployments in the Gulf region during tensions involving Iran. Europe’s main existing anti-ballistic platform, the Franco-Italian SAMP/T system, is produced in comparatively limited volumes.

Separately, Fire Point, the Ukrainian company behind the Flamingo cruise missile, told Reuters earlier this month it is working with European firms on a new lower-cost air defense system that could be introduced next year.

data.title: Options traders shift from war fears to chasing tech rally ahead of earnings
data.description:
Options traders have rapidly moved from hedging against economic fallout from the Iran conflict to positioning for further stock gains, as easing geopolitical tensions and upcoming earnings reports lift risk appetite, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

Oil prices have retreated following the ceasefire and continued U.S.-Iran talks, helping volatility across equities, bonds and other assets fall back toward pre-conflict levels.

Investors who recently bought index puts and VIX (VIX) calls for protection are now rotating into bullish bets on major technology names scheduled to report results in the coming weeks. Analysts say many traders were underinvested during the market rebound and are now trying to catch up.

Attention is also returning to dispersion trades, where investors bet on individual stock volatility outperforming broader index moves. These strategies typically benefit during earnings season as single-stock reactions widen.

JPMorgan’s Arnaud Jobert said systematic dispersion remains one of the firm’s highest-conviction earnings-season trades because of the income potential and expected stock-specific moves.

Still, strategists warn earnings expectations may be elevated in both the U.S. and Europe, raising the risk that disappointing results could be punished more severely than strong reports are rewarded, according to the report.

data.title: Trump says U.S. Navy seized sanctioned Iranian-flagged ship in Gulf of Oman
data.description:
President Donald Trump said Sunday that the U.S. Navy intercepted and boarded an Iranian-flagged cargo vessel in the Gulf of Oman after the ship allegedly ignored repeated orders to stop.

 

A U.S. guided missile destroyer carried out the operation, according to Trump, who described the vessel as the TOUSKA.

“A US guided missile destroyer ‘intercepted the TOUSKA in the Gulf of Oman, and gave them fair warning to stop,” Trump wrote on a social media post Sunday. “The Iranian crew refused to listen, so our Navy ship stopped them right in their tracks by blowing a hole in the engineroom. Right now, US Marines have custody of the vessel.”

Trump added that the ship is subject to U.S. Treasury sanctions because of what he described as a record of illegal activity.

No immediate comment was available from Iranian officials, and independent confirmation of the incident wasn't immediately available. The Gulf of Oman is a strategic waterway connecting the Arabian Sea with the Strait of Hormuz, a vital route for global energy shipments.

For investors, Trump’s statement is significant in signaling the conflict may be expanding from missile strikes and diplomacy into direct maritime enforcement in one of the world’s most critical shipping lanes, raising the risk of retaliation and further disruption to trade. Any escalation in the Gulf of Oman or nearby Strait of Hormuz can quickly move oil prices, inflation expectations and equities, which helps explain the recent swings in global markets.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Supreme Court weighs limits on SEC’s disgorgement power
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court will hear arguments Monday in a case that could further restrict the Securities and Exchange Commission’s ability to force defendants to surrender alleged illegal profits through “disgorgement,” one of the agency’s most important enforcement tools, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

The court has already narrowed the SEC’s authority several times in the past decade. A new ruling could affect cases where victims are difficult to identify, including record-keeping violations, insider trading claims and the agency’s lawsuit against Elon Musk over his delayed disclosure of a growing Twitter stake.

The SEC won more than $6 billion in disgorgement orders in fiscal 2024 and nearly $11 billion in 2025, according to the report.

Supporters say the remedy prevents misconduct by stripping wrongdoers of profits. Critics argue the SEC often uses it to raise money for the Treasury rather than compensate harmed investors. The agency’s 2025 financial report showed more than $5 billion in collected penalties and disgorgement had not yet been distributed to victims.

The case centers on Ongkaruck Sripetch, whom the SEC accused of penny-stock “pump and dump” schemes involving at least 20 companies that allegedly generated $6.6 million in illicit gains. A judge ordered him to pay back $3.3 million plus interest.

Sripetch argues the SEC should only be allowed to seek disgorgement when it can show measurable financial harm to victims. The SEC says the purpose is broader: preventing wrongdoers from keeping unlawful gains.

The justices are expected to rule by July in Sripetch v. SEC.

data.title: NSA is said to use Anthropic’s new AI model despite Pentagon dispute
data.description:
The National Security Agency is using Anthropic’s latest advanced artificial intelligence model, Mythos Preview, even as senior Pentagon officials continue to label the company a potential supply-chain risk, Axios reported Sunday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The reported use highlights growing tension inside the U.S. government over how to balance security concerns with the race to adopt leading AI tools for cyber defense and intelligence work. While the NSA is using Mythos Preview, the model has also seen broader use within the Defense Department.

Anthropic (ANTHRO) is privately held, but it is widely viewed as one of the most important AI developers alongside OpenAI (OPENAI) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL). Any sign that U.S. intelligence agencies are adopting its most advanced systems could strengthen Anthropic’s standing in the lucrative government market and underscore demand for frontier AI models. The episode also shows how national security demand may accelerate commercialization of advanced AI across both public and private sectors.

The model’s exact use by the NSA wasn't clear. Organizations granted access have reportedly used it to scan internal systems for exploitable cybersecurity weaknesses.

Anthropic (ANTHRO) limited Mythos access to roughly 40 organizations, citing concerns that its offensive cyber capabilities were too sensitive for a broader rollout. Only a portion of those users were publicly identified. One source said the NSA was among the previously undisclosed government users.

In Britain, officials have said the U.K. AI Security Institute also has access to the model.

The development comes after Anthropic Chief Executive Dario Amodei met White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Friday to discuss government use of Mythos, along with the company’s broader strategy and security controls, sources said.

People familiar with the talks described the meeting as constructive and said future discussions may focus on adoption by agencies outside the Pentagon, Axios reported.

The friction between Anthropic and the Pentagon was said to emerge during contract negotiations earlier this year. Defense officials wanted Anthropic’s Claude model available for all lawful uses, while the company sought limits around domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons applications.

Some officials remain skeptical of Anthropic’s willingness to support military needs, while others inside the administration are pushing to move past the dispute and gain access to cutting-edge AI capabilities.

data.title: Amneal rated Buy in new research coverage at UBS as growth pipeline expands
data.description:
UBS on Friday initiated coverage of Amneal Pharmaceuticals (AMRX) with a Buy rating and a $19 price target, saying the stock offers one of the strongest growth profiles in specialty pharmaceuticals and could see a valuation re-rating after a recent pullback.

Shares had fallen about 14% over the prior two months, which UBS said created an attractive entry point for investors looking for a mix of stable base revenue and faster-growing products.

Parkinson’s portfolio seen as key growth driver

UBS analysts led by Ashwani Verma expect Amneal’s (AMRX) specialty segment to post a 6% compound annual growth rate from 2025 through 2030, ahead of the 4% consensus estimate. The firm pointed to strong uptake of Crexont, a Parkinson’s treatment launched in late 2024, and a slower-than-expected decline in older drug Rytary.

The bank estimates Crexont could generate about $440 million in sales by 2030, above Street forecasts near $390 million. UBS also said newly launched migraine therapy Brekiya could eventually reach roughly $80 million in peak sales.

Generics and biosimilars pipeline adds upside

UBS forecasts Amneal’s (AMRX) affordable medicines unit, which includes generics and biosimilars, will grow 9% annually through 2030, above the 7% consensus view. Analysts highlighted launches including iohexol, bimatoprost, sodium oxybate and a biosimilar version of Xolair.

The report also said upcoming patent expirations across branded pharmaceuticals could open an estimated $80 billion market opportunity for generic and biosimilar competitors through the next several years.

Margin expansion and lower leverage expected

UBS expects branded product growth to help expand operating margins by 300 basis points over five years. The bank also sees leverage improving, with net debt to ebitda falling below 3 times in the near term.

Valuation still modest, UBS says

At roughly 11 times expected 2027 earnings, UBS said the current valuation implies only modest future growth and does not fully reflect Amneal’s (AMRX) branded portfolio or upgraded generics strategy. Its $19 target is based on a 15 times multiple of projected 2027 earnings.

data.title: Blue Origin’s New Glenn reuses booster but misses target orbit
data.description:
Blue Origin launched its New Glenn rocket on its third mission Sunday, successfully reusing a booster for the first time, but the payload failed to reach its planned orbit.

The rocket lifted off from Cape Canaveral, Florida, at about 7:25 a.m. local time. Roughly 10 minutes later, the reusable first-stage booster landed on a barge in the Atlantic Ocean.

Blue Origin said the satellite separated from the upper stage but entered an “off-nominal orbit,” meaning it was placed incorrectly. The company said the spacecraft has powered on and is being evaluated.

The mission carried a satellite for AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), which is building a direct-to-cellphone communications network. The Texas-based company had seven satellites in orbit entering 2026 and has said it hopes to launch as many as 60 this year.

The setback could complicate Blue Origin’s effort to establish New Glenn as a credible rival to SpaceX’s Falcon 9. The heavy-lift rocket has faced years of delays and slower-than-expected launch cadence.

Chief Executive Dave Limp said before the mission that Blue Origin hoped to complete eight to 12 launches this year, citing strong demand for satellite deployment.

Blue Origin managed only two New Glenn launches last year after initially projecting six to eight.

Beyond launch services, the company is expanding into satellite connectivity, orbital data centers and lunar missions. Limp also said Blue Origin’s Mark 1 lunar lander has “a very good chance” of reaching the moon later this year.
